question_nl: When would have been the last time Manoa Valley might have experienced flooding? Consider rainfall and soil moisture index.
draft_sql:
-- Heavy rainfall days (>50mm) at Manoa-area stations with concurrent soil moisture
WITH manoa_heavy_days AS (
  SELECT d.date_hst, d.station_id, s.name,
    ROUND(d.rainfall_mm::numeric, 1) AS rainfall_mm
  FROM mv_daily_station_summary d
  JOIN mesonet_stations s ON s.station_id = d.station_id
  WHERE d.station_id IN ('0501','0502','0503','0504','0505','0506')
    AND d.rainfall_mm > 50
),
soil AS (
  SELECT date_trunc('day', timestamp)::date AS day,
    ROUND(AVG(value)::numeric, 3) AS avg_vwc
  FROM mesonet_measurements
  WHERE station_id = '0501' AND var_id = 'SM_1_Avg' AND value IS NOT NULL AND value < 1
  GROUP BY date_trunc('day', timestamp)::date
)
SELECT h.date_hst, h.station_id, h.name, h.rainfall_mm,
  s.avg_vwc AS soil_vwc_lyon,
  ROUND((s.avg_vwc * 100)::numeric, 1) AS soil_vwc_pct
FROM manoa_heavy_days h
LEFT JOIN soil s ON s.day = h.date_hst
ORDER BY h.rainfall_mm DESC
LIMIT 30;

-- Supporting queries used in analysis:

-- Multi-day rainfall context at Lyon Arboretum around top events:
-- Mar 10-15 2026: 2.0 + 64.3 + 52.1 + 197.6 + 59.2 + 2.8 = 376mm over 4 days
-- Jan 23-28 2025: 0 + 0 + 0 + 195.3 + 22.4 + 0.3 = 218mm (single-day burst)

-- Soil moisture trajectory at Lyon Arboretum:
-- Pre-March 13: 64% VWC (already saturated from 2 prior rain days)
-- Pre-Jan 26:   46% VWC (drier, soil could absorb rainfall)

-- Peak hourly intensity at Lyon Arboretum:
-- March 13: 16.8 mm/hr sustained over multiple hours
-- Jan 26:   14.7 mm/hr concentrated in one hour
notes: Corrected after initial analysis missed March 23 event. Daily totals are misleading; hourly intensity and lower-valley stations are critical for urban flood assessment.
intent: Identify the most recent probable flooding event in Manoa Valley by combining rainfall intensity, multi-day accumulation, antecedent soil moisture, and watershed-wide conditions
scientific_context: Flash flooding depends on rainfall intensity AND antecedent soil moisture. Saturated soil (VWC >60%) has zero infiltration capacity, so all rainfall becomes surface runoff. Multi-day rain events that saturate soil before the heaviest day produce worse flooding than single-day bursts on dry soil.
reasoning_sketch: 1. Find heavy rainfall days (>50mm) at Manoa-area stations 2. For top candidates, check antecedent soil moisture (SM_1_Avg) in preceding days 3. Check multi-day rainfall context (was it a prolonged event?) 4. Check hourly intensity for flash flood potential 5. Check upstream watershed stations for simultaneous heavy rain 6. Compare events holistically — the worst flood is the one with saturated soil + heavy rain + upstream drainage
expected_answer_shape: Identification of a specific date with supporting evidence from multiple variables
pitfalls: Similar daily rainfall totals can have very different flood outcomes depending on soil moisture
Must consider upstream watershed, not just the valley floor station
Hourly intensity matters more than daily totals for flash floods
No stream gauge data to confirm actual flooding
Ranking by daily rainfall totals misses flash flood events with lower totals but extreme hourly intensity
Must check lower-valley stations (not just upper watershed) to assess campus/urban flooding
follow_up_questions: Were there actual flood reports on March 13, 2026?
How does this compare to the 2004 Manoa flood?
What soil moisture threshold reliably predicts flooding in this watershed?
assumptions: Lyon Arboretum soil moisture is representative of the Manoa watershed
VWC > 60% indicates near-saturation for Hawaiian soils
Stations 0501-0506 collectively represent the Manoa/Nuuanu drainage basin
